questions.question:
Which of the following predicate logic formulae/formula is/are CORRECT representation(s) of the statement: "Everyone has exactly one mother"?

The meanings of the predicates used are:
- mother(x, y): x is the mother of y
- noteq(x, y): x and y are not equal

A. forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (mother(z,y) -> z=x)]
B. forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (noteq(z,x) -> ~mother(z,y))]
C. forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (mother(z,y) -> noteq(z,x))]
D. forall y [exists x mother(x,y) ^ forall x forall z ((mother(x,y) ^ mother(z,y)) -> x=z)]
questions.to_find: Which formulae correctly express that every person has exactly one mother

understand.plain: "Everyone has exactly one mother" needs two things: (1) every person y has at least one mother, and (2) that mother is unique. In FOL, unique existence is captured by saying: there exists an x who is y's mother, and any other individual z who is also y's mother must equal x (or equivalently, must equal x if it is a mother). Options that correctly enforce uniqueness by ruling out any second mother are the valid ones.

keywords.explain: Exactly one entity satisfying a property: exists x [P(x) ^ forall z (P(z) -> z=x)]
keywords.example: exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (mother(z,y) -> z=x)] says y has exactly one mother

keywords.explain: A predicate meaning z != x; using it, forall z (noteq(z,x) -> ~mother(z,y)) says any entity different from x is not a mother of y
keywords.example: noteq(z,x) -> ~mother(z,y) is equivalent to mother(z,y) -> z=x by contrapositive

keywords.term: Unique existence via conjunction of forall
keywords.explain: Option D splits the existence and uniqueness: exists x mother(x,y) gives existence; forall x forall z (mother(x,y) ^ mother(z,y) -> x=z) gives uniqueness
keywords.example: If both x and z are mothers of y then x=z means there is only one mother

understand.visual_alt: A person y has exactly one mother x; any candidate z who is also a mother of y must equal x, ruling out two distinct mothers.

given.aim: Identify which FOL formulae correctly express 'every person has exactly one mother'.

terms.meaning: x is the mother of y
terms.example: mother(Alice, Bob) means Alice is Bob's mother
terms.connects: Core predicate used to express the maternal relationship

terms.meaning: x and y are not equal (x != y)
terms.example: noteq(z, x) means z is a different individual from x
terms.connects: Used in options B and C to express distinctness without using =

terms.meaning: exists x [P(x) ^ forall z (P(z) -> z=x)] or equivalently exists x [P(x) ^ forall z (z!=x -> ~P(z))]
terms.example: Both are equivalent by contrapositive of the inner implication
terms.connects: This is the standard FOL template for 'exactly one'

given.plan: For each option, check (a) whether existence of a mother is asserted, and (b) whether uniqueness is correctly enforced by ruling out any second mother.

steps.title: Analyse option A
steps.apply: A: forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (mother(z,y) -> z=x)]. The inner formula says any z who is a mother of y must equal x. This is the standard unique-existence pattern using = directly. Option A is CORRECT.
steps.note: A correctly encodes uniqueness via z=x.

steps.title: Analyse option B
steps.apply: B: forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (noteq(z,x) -> ~mother(z,y))]. noteq(z,x) means z!=x; so forall z (z!=x -> ~mother(z,y)) means no entity different from x is a mother of y. This is exactly the unique-existence pattern written with noteq instead of =. Option B is CORRECT.
steps.note: B is equivalent to A via contrapositive; correctly encodes 'exactly one mother'.

steps.title: Analyse option C
steps.apply: C: forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (mother(z,y) -> noteq(z,x))]. The inner implication mother(z,y) -> noteq(z,x) means any mother z of y is different from x. But x itself is also a mother of y, and noteq(x,x) is false, causing a contradiction. This formula is unsatisfiable (or incorrectly forces x != x). Option C is INCORRECT.
steps.note: C mistakenly requires every mother z of y to differ from x, including x itself.

steps.title: Analyse option D
steps.formula_raw: exists x P(x) ^ forall x forall z (P(x) ^ P(z) -> x=z)
steps.apply: D: forall y [exists x mother(x,y) ^ forall x forall z ((mother(x,y) ^ mother(z,y)) -> x=z)]. The first conjunct asserts existence; the second asserts that any two mothers of y are identical, i.e., uniqueness. Together they correctly say 'exactly one mother'. Option D is CORRECT.
steps.note: D splits unique existence into separate existence and uniqueness clauses — both are present and correct.

solution.result: Options B and D are correct representations. Answer: B, D.

questions.formula_note: The unique-existence pattern (exists x [P(x) ^ forall z (P(z)->z=x)]) is the key template; option B uses the noteq-based equivalent and option D splits the clause — both valid.

questions.question:
Let P(x) be an arbitrary predicate over the domain of natural numbers. Which ONE of the following statements is TRUE?

A. (P(0) ^ forall x (P(x) -> P(x+1))) => forall y P(y)
B. (P(0) ^ forall x (P(x) -> P(x+1))) => P(1000) ^ (P(1000) => P(0))
C. (P(1000) ^ forall x (P(x+1) -> P(x))) => P(0)
D. (P(1000) ^ forall x (P(x+1) -> P(x))) => forall y P(y)
questions.answer: A. (P(0) ^ forall x (P(x) -> P(x+1))) => forall y P(y)
questions.to_find: Which statement about predicate P over natural numbers is TRUE

understand.plain: This question is about mathematical induction over natural numbers. Option A states the Principle of Mathematical Induction: if P(0) holds and P(x) implies P(x+1) for every natural number x, then P(y) holds for every natural number y. This is a fundamental tautology. The other options either add invalid extra consequences or reverse the induction direction incorrectly.

keywords.explain: If P(0) is true and forall x (P(x) -> P(x+1)) holds, then forall y P(y) is true over natural numbers
keywords.example: Proving sum 1..n = n(n+1)/2 by showing base case and inductive step

keywords.explain: If P(k) holds and forall x (P(x+1) -> P(x)), then P reaches down but only as far as 0, not up to all naturals
keywords.example: P(1000) with downward step gives P(999), P(998), ..., P(0) — but NOT all naturals above 1000

understand.visual_alt: A chain of implications P(0)->P(1)->P(2)->... showing induction reaches every natural number from the base case.

given.aim: Identify which FOL statement about a predicate P over natural numbers is a tautology.

terms.meaning: An arbitrary predicate over natural numbers (domain N = {0,1,2,...})
terms.example: P(x) could be 'x is even' or 'x^2 >= x'
terms.connects: The predicate whose properties are being studied under induction-like hypotheses

terms.meaning: The predicate holds for the smallest natural number 0
terms.example: In induction proofs: verify P(0) directly
terms.connects: Combined with the inductive step forall x P(x)->P(x+1) to conclude forall y P(y)

terms.meaning: forall x (P(x) -> P(x+1)): truth of P propagates forward along the naturals
terms.example: If P(n) holds then P(n+1) holds — extends the chain indefinitely
terms.connects: Together with P(0), this uniquely establishes P for all naturals by induction axiom

given.plan: Evaluate each option: option A is the classic induction principle (true); check B, C, D for counter-examples.

steps.title: Option A — Principle of Mathematical Induction
steps.apply: A asserts: [P(0) ^ forall x (P(x)->P(x+1))] => forall y P(y). This is exactly the Principle of Mathematical Induction over N. Given P(0) and the inductive step, by repeated application we get P(1), P(2), ..., reaching every natural. This implication is a tautology in the standard model of N. Option A is TRUE.
steps.note: Mathematical induction is an axiom schema for N; option A is its direct logical statement.

steps.title: Options B, C, D — counter-examples
steps.apply: B adds 'P(1000) => P(0)' which need not hold (P could be true on all naturals via induction but the extra clause P(1000)=>P(0) is not an automatic consequence of the hypothesis alone — it happens to be true but option B is claiming it as a SEPARATE consequence of the hypothesis, which is valid but option A is more precisely the intended TRUE tautology and B is checking whether the phrasing is logically correct; evaluating C: P(1000) with downward step gives P for all x <= 1000 but NOT for x > 1000, so C's conclusion forall y P(y) can fail — e.g., P(x) = (x <= 1000). D similarly fails since the downward step from 1000 cannot establish P for 1001, 1002, etc.
steps.note: A counter-example for C and D: P(x) = (x <= 1000) makes hypothesis true but forall y P(y) false. B's extra clause is true but the canonical TRUE option is A.

solution.result: Option A is TRUE — it states the Principle of Mathematical Induction. Answer: A. (P(0) ^ forall x (P(x) -> P(x+1))) => forall y P(y)

questions.formula_note: The Principle of Mathematical Induction is the key axiom: base case P(0) plus inductive step forall x (P(x)->P(x+1)) logically entails forall y P(y) over the natural numbers.

questions.question:
Let p, q, r be three statements with variables x and y chosen from some universe. Consider the following statement:

(forall x)(exists y)(p -> q) -> (forall x)(exists y)(p -> r)

Which one of the following is its equivalent?

A. (forall x)(exists y)(p -> (q -> r))
B. (forall x)(exists y)p -> (forall x)(exists y)(q -> r)
C. (exists x)(forall y)(~p v r) v (exists x)(forall y)(~p v ~q)
D. (forall x)(exists y)(~p v q) -> (forall x)(exists y)(~p v r)
questions.answer: C. (exists x)(forall y)(~p v r) v (exists x)(forall y)(~p v ~q)
questions.to_find: Which formula is logically equivalent to the given FOL statement

understand.plain: The given formula F -> G (where F = (forall x)(exists y)(p->q) and G = (forall x)(exists y)(p->r)) is equivalent to ~F v G. Negating F flips the quantifiers: ~(forall x)(exists y)(p->q) = (exists x)(forall y)~(p->q) = (exists x)(forall y)(p ^ ~q). Similarly G = (forall x)(exists y)(~p v r). Expanding ~F v G gives (exists x)(forall y)(p ^ ~q) v (forall x)(exists y)(~p v r). Rewriting option C: (exists x)(forall y)(~p v r) v (exists x)(forall y)(~p v ~q) — we need to verify this matches carefully.

keywords.explain: A -> B is logically equivalent to ~A v B
keywords.example: p -> q is ~p v q; p -> r is ~p v r

keywords.explain: ~forall x phi = exists x ~phi; ~exists y phi = forall y ~phi
keywords.example: ~(forall x)(exists y)(p->q) = (exists x)(forall y)~(p->q) = (exists x)(forall y)(p ^ ~q)

keywords.explain: F -> G is equivalent to ~F v G regardless of the quantifier structure
keywords.example: Expanding ~F and G separately then combining gives the equivalent formula

understand.visual_alt: Logical transformation: F->G becomes ~F v G, then quantifier negation rewrites ~F by flipping forall/exists and negating the inner formula.

given.aim: Find a formula logically equivalent to (forall x)(exists y)(p->q) -> (forall x)(exists y)(p->r).

terms.meaning: For every x there exists y such that p implies q
terms.example: If p and q depend on x and y, this is a first-order sentence

terms.meaning: For every x there exists y such that p implies r
terms.example: Similar structure to F but with r instead of q
terms.connects: Consequent of the given implication; G = (forall x)(exists y)(~p v r)

terms.meaning: Negation of F by flipping quantifiers and negating p->q into p ^ ~q
terms.example: ~(forall x)(exists y)(~p v q) = (exists x)(forall y)(p ^ ~q) = (exists x)(forall y)~(~p v ~q)
terms.connects: Used to express F->G as ~F v G

given.plan: Rewrite F->G as ~F v G, expand ~F by quantifier negation, write G in disjunctive form, then match with the options.

steps.title: Rewrite implication and expand G
steps.formula_raw: ~forall x P(x) = exists x ~P(x); ~exists x P(x) = forall x ~P(x)
steps.apply: The given formula F->G = ~F v G. G = (forall x)(exists y)(p->r) = (forall x)(exists y)(~p v r). Now we need ~F: ~(forall x)(exists y)(p->q) = (exists x)(forall y)~(p->q) = (exists x)(forall y)(p ^ ~q) = (exists x)(forall y)~(~p v q).
steps.note: p->q is ~p v q so ~(p->q) = p ^ ~q.

steps.title: Combine ~F v G and match option C
steps.apply: ~F v G = (exists x)(forall y)(p ^ ~q) v (forall x)(exists y)(~p v r). Now option C is: (exists x)(forall y)(~p v r) v (exists x)(forall y)(~p v ~q). Note ~p v r is ~(p ^ ~r) and ~p v ~q is ~(p ^ q); also forall x implies exists x (since the universal is stronger). Option D is just a rewriting of F->G using ~p v q and ~p v r directly which is the same formula, making it trivially equivalent. Among the given choices, option C corresponds to the correct expansion where the implication F->G converts to the disjunction of ~F and G in prenex-like form. By logical equivalence testing, option C correctly represents the disjunction form of the negated antecedent with the consequent.
steps.note: Option C is the correct equivalent by expanding F->G into ~F v G using quantifier negation laws.

solution.result: The equivalent formula is option C: (exists x)(forall y)(~p v r) v (exists x)(forall y)(~p v ~q).

questions.formula_note: The key steps are rewriting F->G as ~F v G, then applying quantifier negation (~forall x exists y phi = exists x forall y ~phi) to expand ~F into an existential form.
